coredata.dc:title: Determination of metanil yellow dye in turmeric powder using a unique fluorescence Europium doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Abdel-Lateef, Mohamed A.
Albalawi, Marzough Aziz
Al-Ghamdi, Sameera N.
Mahdi, Wael A.
Alshehri, Sultan
El Hamd, Mohamed A.
coredata.dc:description: Turmeric, a spice known for its therapeutic benefits, is a major source of curcumin which is a polyphenol with anti-inflammatory properties. It aids in treating arthritis, anxiety, metabolic syndrome, liver disease, hyperlipidemia, and inflammatory diseases. In this study, a novel fluorescence probe was designed to detect the adulteration of curcumin by metanil yellow (a harmful artificial dye). The probe was synthesized from the carbonization and conversion of the Tannic acid-Eu3+ complex to bright fluorescence Eu-carbon dots in the presence of orthophosphoric acid. The size, morphological, and optical features of the formed Eu-carbon dots were characterized by UV, SEM, TEM, and FTIR techniques. Furthermore, the formed Eu-carbon dots possess unique fluorescence excitation and emission features at 307.5 nm and 340.6 nm, respectively. These fluorescence features can be successfully quenched upon the addition of metanil yellow dye. The value of quenching in the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-C-dots was directly proportional to the dye's concentration. The LOD value for the proposed method was 0.390 µg/mL with a linear range of 1.0–15.0 µg/mL. Furthermore, the methodology exhibited good recovery values for determining the studied dye without any interference from the presence of curcumin.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Curcumin
Carbon dots
Europium
Fluorescence quenching
Metanil yellow
Turmeric powder

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of MTY in turmeric powder
Absorbance and fluorescence spectra of Eu-C-dots
Stability of Eu-C-dots against time and UV irradiation
Fluorescence response of Eu-C-dots towards MTY
Mechanism of fluorescence quenching by MTY
Effect of pH in FI of Eu-C-dots and the quenching by MTY
Analytical parameters for the developed method
Application for the determination of MTY in curcumin powder

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Chemical structure for curcumin, Metanil yellow and Tannic acid.
Characterization of the synthetized Eu-C-dots. (A) for SEM; (B) for TEM, (C) for FTIR spectrum, and (D) for the absorbance spectrum.

(A) Excitaion and emission fluorescence spectra (a and b) for undoped C-dots and (c and d) for Eu-C-dots, respectively, (B) photographs for Eu-C-dots, (I) under daylight and (II) under UV lamp at 312 nm.

(A) Quenching the excitation and emission fluorescence spectra of Eu-C-dots by MTY, (B) (B) photographs for after and before adding of MTY to Eu-C-dots under UV lamp, (C) effect of pH on the FI of Eu-C-dots (blue line) and on the fluorescence quenching (Fo-F) of Eu-C-dots by MTY (red line). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Analytical parameters for the determination of MTY by the proposed method.
Selectivity study and the determination of MTY in Turmeric powder by the proposed method.

head.ce:title: Determination of metanil yellow dye in turmeric powder using a unique fluorescence Europium doped carbon dots

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Al-Azhar University, Assiut Branch, Assiut 71524, Egypt (M. A. Abdel-Lateef), and Department of Pharmaceutical Sciences, College of Pharmacy, Shaqra University, Shaqra 11961, Saudi Arabia (M.A. El Hamd).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Determination of Metanil yellow dye using fluorescent Europium doped Carbon dots.

Turmeric, a spice known for its therapeutic benefits, is a major source of curcumin which is a polyphenol with anti-inflammatory properties. It aids in treating arthritis, anxiety, metabolic syndrome, liver disease, hyperlipidemia, and inflammatory diseases. In this study, a novel fluorescence probe was designed to detect the adulteration of curcumin by metanil yellow (a harmful artificial dye). The probe was synthesized from the carbonization and conversion of the Tannic acid-Eu3+ complex to bright fluorescence Eu-carbon dots in the presence of orthophosphoric acid. The size, morphological, and optical features of the formed Eu-carbon dots were characterized by UV, SEM, TEM, and FTIR techniques. Furthermore, the formed Eu-carbon dots possess unique fluorescence excitation and emission features at 307.5 nm and 340.6 nm, respectively. These fluorescence features can be successfully quenched upon the addition of metanil yellow dye. The value of quenching in the fluorescence intensity of the Eu-C-dots was directly proportional to the dye's concentration. The LOD value for the proposed method was 0.390 µg/mL with a linear range of 1.0–15.0 µg/mL. Furthermore, the methodology exhibited good recovery values for determining the studied dye without any interference from the presence of curcumin.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: In today's world, adulterating food is one of the biggest problems. The food of developing nations, such as Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, India, Afghanistan, and others, is mixed with many spices that provide color and flavour to the food [1]. Since the natural forms of these spices are expensive, they are often adulterated with cheaper colorant dyes. The most common method of food adulteration is the addition of colors, whether those colors are permitted or not permitted. It indicates that everything that is served or ingested in one way or another is made unhealthy for ingestion by adding certain toxic colors and chemical dyes, which are very harmful to human health. Some of these artificial colors are carcinogenic, which means that they pose a health risk to humans if they are taken in over an extended period of time [2]. The use of colors that are not authorized can have significant negative effects not only on public health and society as a whole but also on the economy of countries all over the world where food is produced.

Turmeric (bright yellow color) is extracted from Curcuma longa rhizomes (family Zingiberaceae). Turmeric exists as a medicinal plant and is packaged and merchandised as fresh powder, oil, and dry rhizomes, in the worldwide spice industry. The color of turmeric is attributed to its active principle curcumin (Fig. 1 ) which serves as an indicator of the quality of the turmeric [3]. Due to curcumin's high medicinal potential activity coupled with no actual side effects, curcumin has gotten much attention in recent years [4,5]. Curcumin has been proven to have antioxidant properties, antibacterial, antiviral, antifungal, anti-inflammatory, antitumor activities, and immunomodulatory [6–9]. In addition, studies have shown that curcumin can regulate classic and alternative nervous system pathways and is utilized in the medication for multiple sclerosis, dementia, and Alzheimer's diseases [10–12]. Curcumin is commonly adulterated in Asian countries for financial gain with non-permitted dyes like Sudan III and metanil yellow [13].

Metanil yellow (MTY) Fig. 1 is a highly poisonous and mutagenic azo dye [13]. Their excess use, beyond a safe permissible limit, in the colored food can produce hyperactivity, liver damage, anaemia, allergies, infertility, congenital disabilities, and cancers [13–15]. Various analytical techniques have been effectively utilized to detect MTY in turmeric powder and different food samples. These techniques included; FT-Raman and FT-infrared spectroscopy [16–18], spectrophotometry [19,20], liquid chromatography [1], TLC [21,22] and electrochemical technique[13]. Fluorescence spectroscopy is one of the spectrometric techniques that offers sensitivity, simplicity, cost-effectiveness and selectivity without sacrificing precision [23,24]. To our knowledge, no spectrofluorometric methodology has been reported to screen the presence of MTY in turmeric powder or any samples.

Carbon dots are tiny nano sizes of carbon nanoparticles (less than 10 nm). They have garnered great interest due to their low potential toxicity, excellent water solubility, facile synthesis, and great biocompatibility [25]. In addition to these properties, carbon dots possess exceptional photoluminescence properties, such as characterized fluorescence emission and tunable fluorescence [26]. So, quenching or recovery (via inhibiting the quenching action) of the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots can be efficiently utilized for quantifying and detecting analytes [26]. The color-emitting of the carbon dots can be tuned by varying the utilized precursors and the applied reaction time [27]. Plants containing organic compounds such as phenolic compounds, glucose, and cellulose are very suitable sources for preparing carbon dots (C-dots) [28].

Tannic acid (Fig. 1) is a natural polyphenolic compound from multiple plants which contains a luxuriant amount of phenolic hydroxyl groups. Tannic acid (TA) can form a polyphenol-metal complex with metals as polydentate ligands, which is attributed to the presence of polyphenol groups in its skeleton [29]. Europium ion (Eu3+) is one of the fluorescent rare earth elements [30] and was previously reported to form a stable complex with TA for biomedical applications [31]. In this work, we aim to prepare Eu3+ doped carbon dots (Eu-C-dots) with novel fluorescence characters from the hydrothermal carbonization of the complex of TA-Eu3+ to detect the adulteration of natural turmeric powder by MTY dye. In addition, the spectrofluorimetric technique will be applied for the first time to detect the artificial MTY dye.
Europium (III) chloride hexahydrate, Tannic acid, curcumin, and MTY were products of SIGMA ALDRICH. Orthophosphoric acid and potassium hydroxide were products of El-Nasr Chemical company.

The fluorescence spectra and UV-absorbance spectra were generated by using an FS2 fluorescence spectrometer device (Scinco Co., Korea) and a UV-1601 UV–vis spectrophotometer device (Shimadzu Co., Japan), respectively. Images of Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM) for the synthesized Eu-C-dots were generated by using a JEM-100CX II electron microscope and a JSM 5400 LV microscope, respectively. Both of them are products of JEOL Ltd. Tokyo, Japan. FTIR spectroscope, Nicolet™ 6700, Thermo Scientific Co., USA, was utilized in the region of 4000 to 400 cm−1 to obtain the FTIR spectrum of the synthesized Eu-C-dots. UV trans-Illuminator lamp, a model of WUV-L50 (312 and 365 nm) (Witeg Labortechnik GmbH, Germany), was used at the wavelength of 312 nm.

The detection of metanil yellow was performed in the acidic medium (in the presence of orthophosphoric acid) at room temperature. One milliliter of the acidic Eu-C-dots solution was added in a series of 10-mL standardized flasks and mixed with the aqueous solution of metanil yellow at different levels falling in the concentration range of 10–50 µg/mL. The flask volume was completed to ten milliliters with ultrapure water and shaken well. The intensities of the fluorescent emission spectra were immediately recorded. The value of quenching in fluorescent intensity of Eu-C-dots was calculated and utilized to construct the calibration curve of the method.

The aqueous curcumin solution was prepared by dissolving 10 mg of curcumin powder in about 5 mL ethanol, then gradually completed with sonication up to 100 mL by distilled water. Different concentrations from the aqueous solution of curcumin were mixed with different concentrations of the aqueous solution of MTY. Mixtures were vortexed for two minutes. Finally, the fluorimetric assay was followed to determine the amount of MTY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MTY in turmeric powder
ce:section.ce:para: In varied ratios, different amounts of MTY powder and curcumin powder were utilized to prepare synthetic mixtures from both powders. The prepared ratios were 1:1, 1:2, 1:5, 2:1, and 5:1 w:w from MTY: curcumin. The synthetic mixtures were separately analyzed by extracting the MTY from the mixture. The powder was vortexed with an adequate amount of ultra-pure water for five minutes. Then the content was centrifugated, and the supernatant was completed to the volumetric volume. Finally, the fluorimetric assay was followed to determine the amount of metanil yellow.

There are two steps in the preparing of Eu-C-dots: (i) formation of the TA- Eu3+ complex and (ii) hydrothermal carbonization of the TA- Eu3+ complex. Tannic acid powder and europium (III) chloride hexahydrate were weighed in a ratio of 3:2, then transferred into a 250-mL glass beaker, and dissolved in a proper amount of ultra-pure water. The mixture was vortexed for 5 min and stirred for 12 h under the effect of magnetic stirring. Then, about 20 mL of concentrated orthophosphoric acid solution was added to the beaker content. Then the beaker was transferred, placed on the hot plate, and heated at 220 °C for 7 h. Phosphoric acid (non-toxic, mild acid) plays multiple functions, including as a passivating agent acting as an acid catalyst to boost the dehydration carbonization process of Tannic acid and an agent for crosslinking in the polymerization process [32,33]. Then, the synthesized Eu-C-dots were cooled up to room temperature and centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 1 h. The supernatant was separated, filtrated using a filtered membrane of 0.45 µm, and kept until needed.

The size and the morphology of the synthesized Eu-C-dots were determined using the TEM and SEM techniques, respectively. The photograph of SEM in Fig. 2 A illustrates the spherical shape of the synthesized Eu-C-dots, and the photograph of TEM in Fig. 2 B exhibits the size of the synthesized Eu-C-dots around 6 nm. The FTIR spectrum depicted in Fig. 2 C enabled the determination of surface functional groups of the synthesized Eu-C-dots. According to the FTIR spectrum depicted in Fig. 2 C, the broad, strong peak at 3440.47 cm−1 coincided with O‑H stretched surface attached to the phenolic group [34]. The peak at 2925.34 cm−1 coincided with the C‑H stretching vibration of aromatic compounds[34]. The appearance of a strong absorbance peak at 1636.20 cm−1, matching the presence of the carbonyl group, and the presence of a C‑O stretching vibration band around 1010 cm−1 added further support to the presence of an oxygen atom in Eu-C-dots [35]. Finally, the appearance of the shoulder peak at 1159.54 cm−1 can be related to the stretching of hydrogen-bonded PO groups from phosphates to the OC stretching of P‑O‑C (aromatic) [36]. Results from the FTIR spectrum exhibited that the surfaces of Eu-C-dots comprised hydrophilic groups (hydroxyl, carbonyl, and phosphate), which increased their hydrophilicity and water solubility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance and fluorescence spectra of Eu-C-dots
ce:section.ce:para:
The findings of recent study results indicate that incorporating metal ions into C-dots can alter the original electronic structure of C-dots [37]. The UV–Visible spectrum of Eu-C-dots is offered in Fig. 2 D. The spectrum represents the maximum absorbance bands of Eu-C-dots in the UV region. The peak at around 260 nm of the spectrum is related to π -
π
∗
the transition of the aromatic
∁ = ∁
band, which is similar to the polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon
∁ = ∁
system. Meanwhile, the shoulder peak at around 315 nm of the spectrum appeared due to n -
π
∗
transition of
∁ = O
band[38].

In the current study, it was found that the incorporation of fluorescent metal such as Eu3+ produces a remarkable change in the optical properties of C-dot. To the best of our knowledge, C-dots emitted in the UV region (200–380 nm) were seldomly reported [39]. The blue-shifted fluorescence excitation and fluorescence emission spectra of Eu-C-dots in comparison to the undoped C-dot of Tannic acid are shown in Fig. 3 A,
λ
ex
and
λ
em
of the undoped C-dots are at 395.9 and 487.2 nm, respectively, while the
λ
ex
and
λ
em
of the Eu-C-dots are at 307.5 and 340.6 nm, respectively. Under a UV lamp of 312 nm, the aqueous solution of Eu-C-dots emitted an obvious bright fluorescence (Fig. 3 B, the photograph II). At the same time, it appeared as a light yellow under daylight (Fig. 3 B, the photograph I). Therefore, Eu-C-dots exhibited shorter
λ
em
over the majority of the reported C-dots. The gained unique photoluminescence merits of the Eu-C-dot mainly contributed to the fluorescence features of Eu3+ ions in the composition of C-dots and the reaction conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of Eu-C-dots against time and UV irradiation
ce:section.ce:para: The stability study for the synthesized Eu-C-dots was performed in the aqueous solution. It was found that the synthesized Eu-C-dots Eu-C-dots didn’t subject to any aggregation during 30 days of monitoring. The aqueous solution remains in the homogeneous state, and the fluorescence intensity (FI) remains without remarked change, which supports the abundant surface functionalization of Eu-C-dots. Hence, the synthesized Eu-C-dots possess an adequate shelf-life in the solution state. To evaluate the tolerance of the synthesized Eu-C-dots toward the photolysis, its solution was exposed to UV irradiation under a UV lamp of 365 nm for one hour. It was found that the luminescence of the synthesized Eu-C-dots remained unchanged during the monitored period, which refers to the good photostability of the synthesized Eu-C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response of Eu-C-dots towards MTY
ce:section.ce:para: The defined and specified excitation/emission spectra of the synthesized Eu-C-dots make it very robust in constructing a functional assay system. Interestingly, the aqueous solution of the synthesized Eu-C-dots was sensitive to MTY. The fluorescence intensities of the synthesized Eu-C-dots for both excitation and emission spectra at 307.5 nm and 340.6 nm, respectively, were remarkably decreased upon the reaction with MTY, Fig. 4 A, and the quenching phenomenon is very evident, as offered in the image of Fig. 4 B. Moreover, the FI of the synthesized Eu-C-dots at 340 nm was gradually diminished upon the gradual increase in the concentration of MTY. Consequently, the synthesized Eu-C-dots have the potential to be developed and utilized as a robust fluorescent nanoprobe for the determination of MTY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of fluorescence quenching by MTY
ce:section.ce:para:
The common probable reasons for quenching the FI of Eu-C-dots upon the addition of MTY dye are due to the reductive electron transfer from Eu-C-dots to MTY and Förster resonance energy transfer between Eu-C-dots and MTY. The mechanism of Förster resonance energy transfer from excited Eu-C-dots to MTY can be excluded due to the absence of the overlap between the fluorescence-emission spectrum of Eu-C-dots at 340 nm and the red absorbance spectrum of MTY (which has red color in the acidic medium, Fig. S1). Furthermore, the quenching efficiency of Eu-C-dots fluorescence by MTY was estimated by Stern-Volmer analysis [35].
F o
F
=
K
SV
Q
+ 1
where F and F are the fluorescence intensities of Eu-C-dots in the presence and the absence of MTY, respectively,
Q
refers to the concentration of MTY and
K
SV
refers to the quenching constant. In this study, we found that the fluorescence intensity ratio (F o /F) in a good linear correlation (r = 0.995) with the concentration of MTY with Stern–Volmer quenching constant of
K
SV
= 10514
M
- 1
.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH in FI of Eu-C-dots and the quenching by MTY
ce:section.ce:para: In order to determine MTY effectively, the optimal analytical conditions to develop the fluorescence sensor were carefully analyzed. Since the synthesized Eu-C-dots are a new material, so we study the effect of pH on the FI of Eu-C-dots without MTY; then, we study the effect of pH on the FI quenching by MTY. The effect of pH on the luminescence of the synthesized Eu-C-dots was examined in acidic, neutral, and alkaline pH using 1.0 M KOH solution and phosphoric acid to obtain the required pH. Unlike the majority of previously reported C-dots, whose luminescence is extinguished at the acidic extreme, the luminescence intensity of the synthesized Eu-C-dots appeared in its full brightness state in the strong acidic medium of phosphoric acid at pH = 2. The fluorescence intensity remained without remarkable change up to pH = 6, then the FI of the synthesized Eu-C-dots was remarkably decreased gradually up to pH = 8.5. Finally, FI of the synthesized Eu-C-dots was fully suppressed at extreme basic medium like the majority of previously reported C-dots, Fig. 4 C. In a similar way, after the addition of MTY to Eu-C-dots, the maximum quenching in the FI of Eu-C-dots was observed at the acidic medium, the quenching efficacy was gradually decreased up to the slightly alkaline medium then fully disappeared at high values of pH, Fig. 4 C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical parameters for the developed method
ce:section.ce:para: The calibration curve for determining MTY was constructed by plotting values of (Fo‑F)/Fo against the corresponding concentrations from MTY. The derived calibration equation is (Fo‑F)/Fo = 0.0179C + 0.0516 combined with a good linear relationship in concentration range of 1.0–15.0 µg/mL and values of 0.9996 and 0.9992 for r and r2, respectively. Herein, Fo and F refer to the fluorescence intensity values at the fluorescence emission of 340.6 nm upon the excitation at 307.5 nm in cases of absence and presence of MTY, and C is the concentration of MTY (µg/mL). Based on LOD = 3.3 × SD/slope rule [40], the LOD for MTY is 0.390 µg/mL. Similarly, LOQ = 10 × SD/slope [42], the LOQ value is 1.183 µg/mL. Furthermore, t-student test and F- test were applied to compare the proposed method with the reported method [19] for the determination of MTY. The obtained values were 0.122 for t value and 1.528 for F (tabulated values at 95 % confidence limit were F = 6.338 and t = 2.306). This indicated to there is no significant difference between the proposed method and the reported method for the determination of MTY in respect to accuracy and precision. Table 1 displays the statistical data and analytical parameters for the present method.

Curcumin is practically insoluble in water [40], while MTY is highly soluble in water [15]. Theoretically, we can expect that there are no interferences from the presence of curcumin in the determination of MTY upon using pure cold water as an extractant solvent. Practically, we vigorously dissolve the curcumin in aqueous, as mentioned in section 2.4, and the recovery data in Table 2 confirm that there is no interference from curcumin at high or low concentration levels.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application for the determination of MTY in curcumin powder
ce:section.ce:para: In order to estimate the practicality of the suggested method, the synthesized Eu-C-dots were applied to detect MTY in real curcumin samples. Since no MTY had been detected in the chosen curcumin samples. So five synthetic mixtures from curcumin and MTY powders were prepared, and a recovery experiment was performed. Data in Table 2 refers to the satisfactory recoveries in the range of 98.88 % to 102.49 % were obtained for determining MTY in synthetic mixtures with RSD values less than 3 %.

In this work, the europium-doped carbon dots were synthesized through the hydrothermal method. The natural and non-toxic Tannic acid was chosen for the first time as a carbon source. The synthesized Eu-C-dots were characterized by different microscopical and optical characterization techniques. The Eu-C-dots exhibited unique excitation and emission fluorescence spectra at 307.5 nm and 340.6 nm, respectively. The synthesized Eu-C-dots were successfully utilized for the first time for the fluorometric detection of the harmful dye, metanil yellow. The remarkable advantages of this method are no hazardous solvent or substance has been used, stable photoluminescence, high stability in the extremely acidic medium, and excellent water solubility. These led to the design of a green, selective, sensitive, environmentally friendly and robust fluorometric method for detecting metanil yellow in Turmeric powder.

Mohamed A. Abdel-Lateef: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Resources, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation. Marzough Aziz Albalawi: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Sameera N. Al-Ghamdi: Data curation, Formal analysis, Software. Wael A. Mahdi: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Sultan Alshehri: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Mohamed A. El Hamd: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Investigation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors are thankful to the Researchers Supporting Project (number RSP2022R516) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.
The authors would like to thank the Deanship of Scientific Research at Shaqra University for supporting this work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.122124.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel micellar chromatographic procedure for the determination of metanil yellow in foodstuffs

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: V. Ashok, N. Agrawal, A. Durgbanshi, J. Esteve-Romero, D. Bose, A novel micellar chromatographic procedure for the determination of metanil yellow in foodstuffs, Anal. Methods 7(21) (2015) 9324-9330.

ce:other-ref.ce:textref: C. Potera, Diet and nutrition: the artificial food dye blues, National Institute of Environmental Health Sciences, 2010.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Impedimetric approach for estimating the presence of metanil yellow in turmeric powder from tunable capacitance measurement

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Das, S. Chakraborty, N.K. Bera, K. Acharya, D. Chattopadhyay, A. Karmakar, S. Chattopadhyay, Impedimetric approach for estimating the presence of metanil yellow in turmeric powder from tunable capacitance measurement, Food Anal. Methods 12(4) (2019) 1017-1027.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of capsaicin, dihydrocapsaicin, and curcumin on copper-induced oxidation of human serum lipids

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K.D. Ahuja, D.A. Kunde, M.J. Ball, D.P. Geraghty, Effects of capsaicin, dihydrocapsaicin, and curcumin on copper-induced oxidation of human serum lipids, J. Agricul. Food Chem. 54(17) (2006) 6436-6439.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of dietary curcumin, capsaicin and garlic on the antioxidant status of red blood cells and the liver in high-fat-fed rats

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Kempaiah, K. Srinivasan, Influence of dietary curcumin, capsaicin and garlic on the antioxidant status of red blood cells and the liver in high-fat-fed rats, Annals of nutrition and metabolism 48(5) (2004) 314-320.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Neuroprotection by spice-derived nutraceuticals: you are what you eat!

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Kannappan, S.C. Gupta, J.H. Kim, S. Reuter, B.B. Aggarwal, Neuroprotection by spice-derived nutraceuticals: you are what you eat!, Molecular neurobiology 44(2) (2011) 142-159.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Curcumin has bright prospects for the treatment of multiple sclerosis
